zh.lifeSummary: 當Albrecht VI., der Weise bzw. der Lahme, Graf von Habsburg, Albrecht II.  als Herzog von Österreich於1298年12月12日出生在瑞士阿高哈布斯堡時，他的父親Albert bzw. Albrecht V. Graf von Habsburg, Albert  I. als Herzog von Österreich und der Steiermark und als Deutscher König43歲，母親Elisabeth Gräfin von Görz35歲。他於1324年2月15日與Johanna Gräfin von Pfirt在瑞士巴瑟爾-斯塔特巴塞爾結婚。他們至少是4個兒子和2個女兒的父母。他去世於1358年8月16日在奧地利維恩享年59歲，埋葬在奧地利尼德洛斯特利希謝布斯加明。

zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Albrecht VI., der Weise bzw. der Lahme, Graf von Habsburg, Albrecht II.  als Herzog von Österreich于1298年12月12日出生在瑞士阿高哈布斯堡时，他的父亲Albert bzw. Albrecht V. Graf von Habsburg, Albert  I. als Herzog von Österreich und der Steiermark und als Deutscher König43岁，母亲Elisabeth Gräfin von Görz35岁。他于1324年2月15日与Johanna Gräfin von Pfirt在瑞士巴瑟爾-斯塔特巴塞爾结婚。他们至少是4个儿子和2个女儿的父母。他去世于1358年8月16日在奥地利維恩享年59岁，埋葬在奥地利尼德洛斯特利希謝布斯加明。

nameMeanings.name: Graf
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Hans, Kurt, Otto, Erwin, Ernst, Fritz, Gerhard, Hermann, Horst, Manfred, Udo, Alois.

German (also Gräf): status name from Middle High German grāve, grābe, which was used as a title denoting various more or less aristocratic dignitaries and officials. In later times it became established as a title of nobility equivalent to the Romance count. The vocabulary word also denoted a variety of different minor local functionaries in different parts of Germany. In the Grand Duchy of Hesse, for example, it was used for the holder of the comparatively humble office of village headman (compare Mayer , Schulz , and Vogt ). The surname could have originated from any of these senses or be a metonymic occupational or status name for a servant or retainer of a count, or a nickname for someone who gave himself airs and graces. This surname is also found in many other European countries, for example in France (Alsace and Lorraine), Hungary, Poland, Czechia, Slovakia, Croatia, and Slovenia, often as a translation into German of the corresponding Hungarian and Slavic surnames Gróf and Grof . Compare Graef .
Jewish (Ashkenazic): artificial name selected, like   Herzog   and other words denoting titles, because of their aristocratic connotations.

variants.name: Grebe
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Kurt, Erwin, Fritz, Hans, Willi.
German (Hesse): variant of   Graf  .

variants.name: De Graaf
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Dutch Gerrit, Adriaan, Bastiaan, Cornelis, Dirk, Geert.

Dutch: from graaf ‘count’ (see Graf ), with the addition of the definite article de ‘the’, hence a nickname for the servant of a count or for someone supposedly resembling one, and also a topographic or habitational name referring to a house or inn named with this word.

variants.name: Graefe
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Armin, Arno, Goetz, Horst, Johannes.
German (North Rhine-Westphalia; also Gräfe): variant of Graf 1.

variants.name: Grefe
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Erhardt, Gerhard, Gerhart, Kurt.
North German: variant of   Graf  .

variants.name: Greve
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Gerhard, Otto, Reinhold, Bernhardt, Claus, Erna, Erwin, Ewald, Fritz, Kurt.
North German and Scandinavian: status name from Middle Low German and Danish greve ‘count’, equivalent to German Graf .
Dutch: vowel variant of Grave ‘count’, or a topographic name from Middle Dutch greve ‘ditch, moat, channel’.

variants.name: Croff
meanings: Altered form of German   Graf   or   Graff  . Or of Dutch   Kroft  .

variants.name: Mayer
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Kurt, Otto, Hans, Franz, Helmut, Wilhelm, Erwin, Gerhard, Heinz, Ilse, Lorenz, Wolfgang.

English (Staffordshire): post-medieval form of Mear, which is either a topographic name, from Middle English mere ‘pond, pool’ (Old English mere) or Middle English mere ‘boundary’ (Old English (ge)mǣre), or a habitational name from one or more of the many places with names derived from these terms, such as Mere (Cheshire, Lincolnshire, Wiltshire), Meir in Caverswall (Staffordshire), and Meare (Somerset).
English: occupational name for a physician, from Old French mire, Middle English mire, meir, meyre, originally synonymous with Myer .

variants.name: Grafe
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Fritz, Ralf.
German (also Gräfe): variant of Graf 1.

variants.name: Graef
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Egon, Lothar, Otto, Armin, Claus, Erwin, Franz, Friedrich, Hans, Helmut, Hermann, Jochen.
German (also Gräf): see Graf .
Dutch: variant of Graaf, see De Graaf .

variants.name: Graff
meanings: German and Jewish (Ashkenazic) (also Gräff): variant of Graf . Compare Graeff .

English (London): metonymic occupational name for a clerk or scribe, from Old French grafe ‘stylus, pencil’, a derivative of Old French grafer ‘to write’ (from Late Latin grafare, from Greek graphein). It is uncertain whether the English name survived the medieval period or whether the early 17th century bearers in Britain were in fact of German origin.

nameMeanings.name: von
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Altered form of Welsh   Vaughan  .
Chinese: variant of   Wong  .
Cambodian: written វ៉ុន, unexplained.

variants.name: Vaughan
meanings: Welsh: from fychan, a lenited form of bychan, a diminutive of bach ‘little’. This was often used to distinguish the younger of two bearers of the same personal name, typically the son of a father with the same name.

nameMeanings.name: Albrecht
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Kurt, Erwin, Hans, Manfred, Otto, Horst, Klaus, Arno, Heinz, Milbert, Fritz, Gerhard.

German: from the personal name Albrecht, composed of the ancient Germanic elements adal ‘noble’ + berht ‘bright, famous’. Compare Albert . This surname is also found elsewhere in central Europe, e.g. in Czechia.
Germanized or Americanized form of Slovenian Albreht: from the personal name Albreht, of ancient Germanic origin (see 1 above).

variants.name: App
meanings: German: from a short form of the personal name Apprecht, a variant of Albrecht . See also Albert .

variants.name: Abbo
meanings: Italian (Liguria): from the personal name Abbo, a variant of the ancient Germanic name Abo, a pet form of Albrecht .
Dutch: from the personal name Abbo, a pet form of Albert .
Muslim: variant of   Abu  . Compare   Abo  .

variants.name: Aber
meanings: German: from a short form of the personal name   Albrecht  .
Americanized form of German and Jewish (Ashkenazic)   Eber  .
Altered form of French Hébert (see Hebert ).

variants.name: Ahlbrecht
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Erwin, Otto, Wulf.
German: variant of   Albrecht  .

variants.name: Abke
meanings: North German: from a pet form of of the personal name   Albrecht  .

variants.name: Albracht
meanings: German: variant of   Albrecht  .

variants.name: Albright
meanings: Americanized form of German   Albrecht  .
English: cognate of Albert , from the Middle English personal name Albriht, Old English Æthelbriht, a metathesized form of Æthelbeorht. Compare Allbright .
History: Jacob Albright (1759–1808), a prominent Methodist preacher, was born in Pottstown, PA, the son of a German immigrant called Johann Albrecht.

variants.name: Abel
meanings: English, German, Dutch, Danish, Norwegian, French, Spanish, and Polish; Slovak (mainly Ábel) and Czech (also Ábel): from the Biblical personal name Abel, Slovak Ábel, which was used in continental Europe from the sixth century. After the Norman Conquest it was introduced to England and Scotland, where it had a brief currency in the 12th and 13th centuries before being revived in England after the Reformation. In the Book of Genesis Abel is a son of Adam, murdered by his brother Cain (Genesis 4:1–8). In Christian tradition he is regarded as a representative of suffering innocence. The Hebrew form of the name is Hebel (Latinized as Abel), from a vocabulary word meaning ‘breath’.
German: from the personal name Abel, a pet form of Albrecht .

variants.name: Aberle
meanings: South German and Swiss German: from a pet form of the personal name   Albrecht  .

variants.name: Albert
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Camille, Fernand, Lucien, Marcel, Normand, Cecile, Adrien, Armand, Henri, Jacques, Alban, Andre.

French, English, German, Catalan, Hungarian, Romanian, Dutch, Slovak, Czech, Croatian, Slovenian, West Indian (mainly Haiti), and Mauritian: from the personal name Albert, composed of the ancient Germanic elements adal ‘noble’ + berht ‘bright, famous’. The standard German form is Albrecht . The name was borne by several Christian saints, notably Saint Albert of Prague, a Bohemian prince who died a martyr in 997 attempting to convert the Prussians to Christianity, and Saint Albert the Great (c. 1193–1280), an Aristotelian theologian and tutor of Thomas Aquinas. In more recent times it has been adopted as a Jewish surname.

nameMeanings.name: II.
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Japanese: written 井伊 ‘well’ and ‘that’, the name one of Japan's great daimyō families, originally based in Tōtōmi (now part of Shizuoka prefecture), later in Kōzuke (now Gunma prefecture) and Ōmi (now Shiga prefecture). Other variants of the name substitute either character with similar sounding characters such as 飯 ‘cooked rice’, 居 ‘residence’, and so forth. It was formerly Romanized as Iyi. — Note: In the population figure published by the US Census Bureau, the Roman number II (meaning ‘the second’, i.e. ‘the younger’ of the two bearers of the name) is apparently also counted as a surname Ii.

History: Ii Naomasa (1561–1602) was awarded lands in Kōzuke and Ōmi for his service in the civil wars that won the shōgunate (military dictatorship) for Tokugawa Ieyasu. His family built the beautiful castle of Hikone, which still stands. His descendant Ii Naosuke (1815–1860) ruled Japan for two years as Tairō (Great Elder) and played a major part in signing treaties that opened Japan to foreign trade at the end of the Tokugawa Shōgunate. He was assassinated by adherents of the anti-foreign party.

nameMeanings.name: als
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Scandinavian Alf, Erik, Sven.
Norwegian (also Ås): habitational name from any of numerous farmsteads so named, from Old Norse áss ‘hill, ridge’.
Swedish (also Ås): topographic name from ås ‘ridge’. Compare Aus , Oas , Ohs , and Oss .

variants.name: Aus
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Scandinavian Alf, Dagny, Erik.
Norwegian and Swedish: variant of   Aas   and, in North America, (also) an altered form of this.

nameMeanings.name: Herzog
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Hans, Erwin, Fritz, Heinz, Kurt, Otto, Wolfgang, Franz, Arno, Ernst, Hildegarde, Reinhold.

German: from the Middle High German title of nobility herzoge ‘duke’ (Old High German herizoho, from heri ‘army’ + ziohan ‘to lead’, a calque of the Byzantine title stratēlatēs ‘general, commander’, from Greek stratos ‘army’ + elaunein ‘to lead’). The name is unlikely to refer to descent from an actual duke; it is normally an occupational name for the servant of a duke or a nickname for one who put on the airs and graces of a duke. This surname is also found in France (Alsace and Lorraine), Poland, Czechia, and Slovenia (see also 3 below). Compare Hertzog .
Jewish (Ashkenazic): artificial name from German Herzog ‘duke’ (see 1 above). Compare Graf and Kaiser .

variants.name: Hartsook
meanings: Americanized form of German   Herzog  .

variants.name: Herceg
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Croatian Stanko.

Croatian: nickname from herceg ‘duke’, a title of the medieval feudal lords of Herzegovina (‘duke's land’, a region in Bosnia and Herzegovina), derived from the Hungarian form of German Herzog . Compare Erceg .

variants.name: Herczeg
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Hungarian Imre, Laszlo, Tibor, Attila, Ferenc, Gaza, Istvan, Janos.
Hungarian form of German   Herzog  .

variants.name: Herzig
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Erna, Fritzi, Hans, Horst, Markus, Monika.
German (eastern and central Germany and Silesia, Switzerland): variant of   Herzog  .
German: from a short form of any of several ancient Germanic personal names composed with hard ‘strong, hardy’, for instance Herto.

variants.name: Hertzog
meanings: German and Jewish (Ashkenazic): variant of Herzog and, in North America, (also) an altered form of this. The surname Hertzog is also found in France (Alsace and Lorraine).

variants.name: Hartzog
meanings: Americanized form of German   Herzog   1.
Jewish (Ashkenazic): variant of   Herzog  .

variants.name: Hartsock
meanings: Probably an altered form of   Herzog  .

variants.name: Hartsoe
meanings: Perhaps an altered form of   Herzog  .

variants.name: Hartsough
meanings: Americanized form of German   Herzog  .

nameMeanings.name: von
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Altered form of Welsh   Vaughan  .
Chinese: variant of   Wong  .
Cambodian: written វ៉ុន, unexplained.

variants.name: Vaughan
meanings: Welsh: from fychan, a lenited form of bychan, a diminutive of bach ‘little’. This was often used to distinguish the younger of two bearers of the same personal name, typically the son of a father with the same name.

nameMeanings.name: Österreich
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Gerhard, Kurt, Uwe, Wolfgang.

German (rarely Österreich) and Jewish (Ashkenazic): habitational name from the province of Upper or Lower Austria, from German Österreich ‘Austria’, the name of which literally means ‘Eastern kingdom’.

variants.name: Oestreicher
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Ernst, Franz, Otto, Horst.  Jewish Shloime, Chaim, Mendy.
German (also Östreicher) and Jewish (Ashkenazic): habitational name for someone from the province of Upper or Lower Austria (see Oesterreich ).

variants.name: Oestreich
meanings: German (also Östreich) and Jewish (Ashkenazic): variant of Oesterreich .

nameMeanings.name: Albrecht
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: from adal ‘noble’ + berht ‘bright’.

nameMeanings.name: VI.,
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Short form of   Violet  ,   Vivien  , or   Vivian .

variants.name: Vivien
meanings: Earlier generally taken as a variant of the boy's name Vivian , but now also rarely used for boys. This spelling was quite common in Old French. Its use as a girl's name in the English-speaking world was influenced by Tennyson's Merlin and Vivien ( 1859 ). This name, from Arthurian legend, may represent an altered form of a Celtic name (perhaps akin to the Irish Gaelic name Béibhinn ‘white lady’, pronounced ‘bee-veen’). The actress Vivien Leigh ( 1913–67 ) was christened Vivian.

variants.name: Violet
meanings: From the name of the flower (Old French violette, Late Latin violetta, a diminutive of viola). This was one of the earliest flower names to become popular in Britain, being used as early as 1700 and becoming well established in the 19th century, although it is now somewhat out of favour.

variants.name: Vivian
meanings: Originally a boy's name, from an Old French form of the Latin name Vivianus (probably a derivative of vivus ‘alive’), but now more frequent as a girl's name. The name was borne by a 5th-century bishop of Saintes in western France, remembered for protecting his people during the invasion of the Visigoths.

nameMeanings.name: der
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Pet form of any of the given names beginning with the letter D- ( compare Jay and Kay ), in particular Dorothy . It is also used as an independent name, and may in some cases be associated with the River Dee ( compare Clyde ).

nameMeanings.name: Weise
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Kurt, Wolfgang, Gerhard, Heinz, Manfred, Wolf, Aloysius, Arno, Dieter, Ewald, Frieda, Fritz.
German and Jewish (Ashkenazic): variant of Weis . Sometimes the name may also be an altered form of Wiese, from Middle High German wise ‘meadow’, which is frequent in placenames.
Altered form of German   Wiese   or   Weiss  .

variants.name: Weis
meanings: ‘Impressive might’.

variants.name: Weiske
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Hans, Kurt.
German:
diminutive of   Weise  .

nameMeanings.name: bzw.
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (Berkshire and Oxfordshire): from Middle English be(a)u, bewe ‘beautiful, fair’ (Old French beu, bel).

variants.name: Bugh
meanings: From an Old French name, Hugues, Hugo, of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from hug ‘heart, mind, spirit’. It was originally a short form of various compound names containing this element. This was borne by the aristocracy of medieval France, adopted by the Normans, and introduced by them to Britain. It was consistently one of the most popular boys' names in the Middle Ages in England, but fell out of favour in the 16th century until it was revived in the 20th century. Little Hugh of Lincoln was a child supposed in the Middle Ages to have been murdered by Jews in about 1255 , a legend responsible for several outbursts of anti-Semitism at various times. The story is referred to by Chaucer in The Prioress's Tale. He is not to be confused with St Hugh of Lincoln ( 1140–1200 ), bishop of Lincoln ( 1186–1200 ), who was noted for his charity and good works, his piety, and his defence of the Church against the State. Since the later Middle Ages Hugh has also been used as an Anglicized form of the Gaelic name Aodh .

nameMeanings.name: der
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Pet form of any of the given names beginning with the letter D- ( compare Jay and Kay ), in particular Dorothy . It is also used as an independent name, and may in some cases be associated with the River Dee ( compare Clyde ).

nameMeanings.name: Lahme,
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Altered form of French (Normandy) Leheup, itself a variant of Lahuppe, from huppe ‘hoopoe’ + the definite article la, a nickname based on this bird's crest or cry (see also Huppe ). The surname Lahue, possibly of different origin, is also found in France, where it is, like the surnames Leheup and Lahuppe, rare. Compare Lehew .
Altered form of French Lagüe (see Lague 1).
History: Moses (Moise) Deforest Lahue, born in 1838 in VT, was a son of Pierre (Peter) Lague from QC.

variants.name: Huppe
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Andre, Armand, Alain, Camille, Cecile, Fernand, Marcel.
French: nickname from huppe ‘hoopoe’ (the bird), based on this bird's cry, or applied to someone with a hair resembling the hoopoe's crest or perhaps with a long curved nose resembling its bill.
French (Huppé): nickname related to 1 above, from the past participle of the Old French verb huper ‘to utter a long cry’.

variants.name: Lague
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Normand, Andre, Laurette, Marie-Ange, Yvon.
French:

(Lagüe): topographic name for someone who lived by a lagoon, from a Gascon dialect form of French lagune ‘lagoon’, or a habitational name from (La) Lagüe, the name of several places in the southwestern part of France. Compare Lahue 2.

variants.name: Lehew
meanings: American French: variant of   Lahue   1.

historicEvents.description: Decisive Battle against the counts of Habsburg.

en.lifeSummary: When Albrecht VI., der Weise bzw. der Lahme, Graf von Habsburg, Albrecht II.  als Herzog von Österreich was born on 12 December 1298, in Habsburg, Aargau, Switzerland, his father, Albert bzw. Albrecht V. Graf von Habsburg, Albert  I. als Herzog von Österreich und der Steiermark und als Deutscher König, was 43 and his mother, Elisabeth Gräfin von Görz, was 35. He married Johanna Gräfin von Pfirt on 15 February 1324, in Basel, Basel-Stadt, Switzerland. They were the parents of at least 4 sons and 2 daughters. He died on 16 August 1358, in Vienna, Austria, at the age of 59, and was buried in Gaming, Scheibbs, Lower Austria, Austria.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Albrecht VI., der Weise bzw. der Lahme, Graf von Habsburg, Albrecht II.  als Herzog von Österreich est né le 12 décembre 1298, à Habsburg, Argovie, Suisse, son père, Albert bzw. Albrecht V. Graf von Habsburg, Albert  I. als Herzog von Österreich und der Steiermark und als Deutscher König, avait 43 ans et sa mère, Elisabeth Gräfin von Görz, avait 35 ans. Il a épousé Johanna Gräfin von Pfirt le 15 février 1324, à Bâle, Bâle-Ville, Suisse. Ils ont eu au moins 4 fils et 2 filles. Il est décédé le 16 août 1358, à Vienne, Autriche, à l’âge de 59 ans, et a été enterré à Gaming, Scheibbs, Niederösterreich, Autriche.

de.lifeSummary: Als Albrecht VI., der Weise bzw. der Lahme, Graf von Habsburg, Albrecht II.  als Herzog von Österreich am 12. Dezember 1298 in Habsburg, Aargau, Schweiz geboren wurde, war sein Vater Albert bzw. Albrecht V. Graf von Habsburg, Albert  I. als Herzog von Österreich und der Steiermark und als Deutscher König 43 und seine Mutter Elisabeth Gräfin von Görz 35. Er hat am 15. Februar 1324 in Basel, Basel-Stadt, Schweiz Johanna Gräfin von Pfirt geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 4 Söhne und 2 Töchter. Er starb am 16. August 1358 im Alter von 59 Jahren in Wien, Österreich und wurde in Gaming, Scheibbs, Niederösterreich, Österreich begraben.

it.lifeSummary: Quando Albrecht VI., der Weise bzw. der Lahme, Graf von Habsburg, Albrecht II.  als Herzog von Österreich è nato il il 12 dicembre 1298 nel Habsburg, Argovia, Svizzera, il suo padre, Albert bzw. Albrecht V. Graf von Habsburg, Albert  I. als Herzog von Österreich und der Steiermark und als Deutscher König, aveva 43 anni e il suo madre, Elisabeth Gräfin von Görz, aveva 35 anni. Lui sposato Johanna Gräfin von Pfirt il 15 febbraio 1324, nel Basilea, Basilea-Città, Svizzera. Erano genitori di almeno 4 figli e 2 figlie. ja.lifeSummary: Albrecht VI., der Weise bzw. der Lahme, Graf von Habsburg, Albrecht II.  als Herzog von Österreichが1298年12月12日にスイスアールガウハブスブルグで生まれた時，父Albert bzw. Albrecht V. Graf von Habsburg, Albert  I. als Herzog von Österreich und der Steiermark und als Deutscher Königは43歳，母Elisabeth Gräfin von Görzは35歳であった。1324年2月15日にスイスバセル-スタトバセルでJohanna Gräfin von Pfirtと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも4人の息子と2人の娘の親であった。彼は1358年8月16日にオーストリアウィンで59歳で死去し，オーストリアニーデルoステレイチシェイブズガミングにて埋葬された。

ko.lifeSummary: Albrecht VI., der Weise bzw. der Lahme, Graf von Habsburg, Albrecht II.  als Herzog von Österreich(이)가 1298년 12월 12일 스위스 아아르가 하브스버그에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Albert bzw. Albrecht V. Graf von Habsburg, Albert  I. als Herzog von Österreich und der Steiermark und als Deutscher König(은)는 43세였고, 그의 어머니 Elisabeth Gräfin von Görz(은)는 35세였다. 그는 1324년 2월 15일 스위스 바셀-스타드트 바셀에서 Johanna Gräfin von Pfirt(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 네 명의 아들과 두 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1358년 8월 16일 오스트리아 빈에서 59세의 나이로 사망했으며 오스트리아 니더o스터레이치 슈이브브스 갬잉에 묻혔다.

pt.lifeSummary: Quando Albrecht VI., der Weise bzw. der Lahme, Graf von Habsburg, Albrecht II.  als Herzog von Österreich nasceu em 12 de dezembro de 1298, em Habsburg, Aargau, Suiça, seu pai, Albert bzw. Albrecht V. Graf von Habsburg, Albert  I. als Herzog von Österreich und der Steiermark und als Deutscher König, tinha 43 anos e sua mãe, Elisabeth Gräfin von Görz, tinha 35 anos. Ele casou-se com Johanna Gräfin von Pfirt em 15 de fevereiro de 1324, em Basel, Basel-Stadt, Suiça. Eles tiveram pelo menos 4 filhos e 2 filhas. Ele faleceu em 16 de agosto de 1358, em Wien, Áustria, com 59 anos, e foi sepultado em Gaming, Scheibbs, Niederösterreich, Áustria.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Albrecht VI., der Weise bzw. der Lahme, Graf von Habsburg, Albrecht II.  als Herzog von Österreich родился 12 декабря 1298 г. в местности хабсбург, ааргау, Швейцария, его отцу по имени Albert bzw. Albrecht V. Graf von Habsburg, Albert  I. als Herzog von Österreich und der Steiermark und als Deutscher König было 43 года, а матери, которую звали Elisabeth Gräfin von Görz, 35. Он женился на женщине по имени Johanna Gräfin von Pfirt 15 февраля 1324 г. в местности беисел, беисел-стадт, Швейцария. У них было по крайней мере 4 сына и 2 дочери. Он умер 16 августа 1358 г. в местности Вена, Австрия в возрасте 59 лет и был похоронен в местности гаминг, щиббс, ниедирoстирраич, Австрия.

Se casó con Johanna Gräfin von Pfirt el 15 de febrero de 1324, en Basel, Basel-Stadt, Suiza. Fueron padres de por lo menos 4 hijos y 2 hijas. Murió el 16 de agosto de 1358, en Wien, Austria, a la edad de 59 años, y fue sepultado en Gaming, Scheibbs, Niederösterreich, Austria.
